title: The Surprising Rise of American Express CEO Stephen Squeri
content:
Stephen Squeri began his professional journey laboring full-time as a stock boy in an elegant department store. Despite being looked down upon by Ivy League trainees, he persevered and eventually joined American Express as a manager in 1985. Over the next three decades, Squeri steadily moved upward into larger roles, primarily focusing on technological operations and commercial cards. However, at almost every level of his ascent, superiors bluntly warned him that he had reached the absolute peak of his career. Despite these discouraging remarks, his undeniable work ethic continually forced his bosses to revise their initial negative assessments.

Squeri’s unexpected path to the presidency accelerated after a series of sudden corporate crises shook the company. The clear front-runner for the CEO position tragically passed away in 2015, leaving a massive leadership vacuum. Shortly afterward, American Express lost its most lucrative corporate partnership, which had accounted for eight percent of its worldwide business. To make matters even worse, a rival credit card company launched a highly popular premium card that threatened Amex’s market dominance. Tasked with managing this multifaceted crisis, Squeri successfully executed a mammoth restructuring initiative that saved the company a billion dollars in operating expenses.

His remarkable success in leading the massive restructuring deeply impressed the outgoing chief executive officer. Recognizing Squeri's extraordinary dedication, the outgoing CEO personally endorsed him as the only logical successor for the top job. However, Squeri still had to convince a highly skeptical board of directors who viewed him merely as an internal operations manager. He embarked on an intensive campaign to individually persuade all fourteen voting members of his strategic vision. Ultimately, Squeri won the prestigious job and successfully steered the historic financial institution into a new era of profitability.
